paper_title: PoseDreamer: Scalable and Photorealistic Human Data Generation Pipeline with Diffusion Models

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Acquiring labeled datasets for 3D human mesh estimation is challenging due to depth ambiguities and the inherent difficulty of annotating 3D geometry from monocular images. Existing datasets are either real, with manually annotated 3D geometry and limited scale, or synthetic, rendered from 3D engines that provide precise labels but suffer from limited photorealism, low diversity, and high production costs. In this work, we explore a third path: generated data. We introduce PoseDreamer, a novel pipeline that leverages diffusion models to generate large-scale synthetic datasets with 3D mesh annotations. Our approach combines controllable image generation with Direct Preference Optimization for control alignment, curriculum-based hard sample mining, and multi-stage quality filtering. Together, these components naturally maintain correspondence between 3D labels and generated images, while prioritizing challenging samples to maximize dataset utility. Using PoseDreamer, we generate more than 500,000 high-quality synthetic samples, achieving a 76% improvement in image-quality metrics compared to rendering-based datasets. Models trained on PoseDreamer achieve performance comparable to or superior to those trained on real-world and traditional synthetic datasets. In addition, combining PoseDreamer with synthetic datasets results in better performance than combining real-world and synthetic datasets, demonstrating the complementary nature of our dataset. We will release the full dataset and generation code.

sections.content: 6 2 0 2 0 3 ] . [ 1 3 6 7 8 2 . 3 0 6 2 : r PoseDreamer: Scalable and Photorealistic Human Data Generation Pipeline with Diffusion Models Lorenza Prospero1,2, Orest Kupyn2, Ostap Viniavskyi3, João F. Henriques2, and Christian Rupprecht2 1 The Podium Institute for Sports Medicine and Technology, University of Oxford 2 Visual Geometry Group, University of Oxford 3 Ukrainian Catholic University {lorenza,okupyn,joao,chrisr}@robots.ox.ac.uk, viniavskyi@ucu.edu.ua https://prosperolo.github.io/posedreamer Fig. 1: PoseDreamer Pipeline: Our method builds high-quality synthetic human dataset using aligned controllable diffusion, hard sample mining, and filtering. It produces photorealistic images with precise spatial control and diverse scenarios for robust training. With fixed SMPL-X [42] parameters, varying only the text caption yields diverse, controllable changes in background, clothing, and environment. Abstract. Acquiring labeled datasets for 3D human mesh estimation is challenging due to depth ambiguities and the inherent difficulty of annotating 3D geometry from monocular images. Existing datasets are either real, with manually annotated 3D geometry and limited scale, or synthetic, rendered from 3D engines that provide precise labels but suffer from limited photorealism, low diversity, and high production costs. In this work, we explore third path: generated data. We introduce PoseDreamer, novel pipeline that leverages diffusion models to generate large-scale synthetic datasets with 3D mesh annotations. Our approach combines controllable image generation with Direct Preference Optimization for control alignment, curriculum-based hard sample mining, and multi-stage quality filtering. Together, these components naturally maintain correspondence between 3D labels and generated images, while prioritizing challenging samples to maximize dataset utility. Using PoseDreamer, we generate more than 500,000 high-quality synthetic samples, achieving 76% improvement in image-quality metrics compared to L. Prospero et al. rendering-based datasets. Models trained on PoseDreamer achieve performance comparable to or superior to those trained on real-world and traditional synthetic datasets. In addition, combining PoseDreamer with synthetic datasets results in better performance than combining realworld and synthetic datasets, demonstrating the complementary nature of our dataset. We will release the full dataset and generation code.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Human recognition tasks in Computer Vision encompass range of methods for detecting and describing humans in images. These range from simpler tasks such as bounding box detection, 2D pose estimation, and semantic segmentation, aimed at identifying the position and semantics of humans in images, to more complex challenges such as 3D detection, 3D pose estimation, and human avatar reconstruction, which seek to unravel the 3D geometry of the human body from images. The success of these methods largely depends on the availability of large labeled datasets for training supervised models. For example, the Microsoft COCO dataset [27] has significantly advanced 2D human pose estimation. However, creating large labeled datasets is costly and time-consuming, limiting their availability for downstream tasks. Labeling 3D data is even more challenging and prone to errors. For instance, labeling 3D body pose from single view is highly ambiguous due to depth ambiguities and occlusions. Similarly, the DensePose dataset [10] incorporated highly sophisticated labeling process to map pixels to surface-based representation of the human body, yet produced only very sparse annotations despite significant human effort. This highlights the need for alternative solutions to obtain images with reliable 3D annotations, particularly for human-centric applications. Advanced rendering engines offer one promising direction, allowing researchers to generate synthetic human datasets with corresponding 3D meshes [3,7,41,60]. However, these approaches face significant practical limitations that restrict their widespread adoption. First, creating photorealistic renders requires substantial technical expertise in 3D modeling, lighting, and material design. Second, building diverse scenes necessitates extensive libraries of 3D assets, including clothing, accessories, backgrounds, and environmental elements, which are expensive to acquire or develop. Third, achieving realistic human appearance requires sophisticated setups for modeling skin textures, hair, facial expressions, and natural deformationsall of which are computationally intensive. Most critically, rendered images often exhibit synthetic look that creates domain gap when models trained on such data are applied to real-world scenarios, limiting their practical effectiveness. In this work, we explore an emerging direction: generated data. Recent advances in diffusion models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in generating photorealistic images across diverse domains. This presents an opportunity to address the limitations of manual annotation and rendering-based approaches PoseDreamer by leveraging the inherent realism and scene diversity of generative models. However, the naive application of diffusion models to data generation faces fundamental challenge: ensuring precise correspondence between generated images and their 3D annotations, requirement that existing generative models do not inherently satisfy. We present PoseDreamer: novel pipeline for generating synthetic datasets for human mesh recovery that harnesses state-of-the-art diffusion models [24] while ensuring precise 3D-2D consistency. We demonstrate that diffusion-based generation is practical alternative to traditional rendering pipelines, offering greater scene diversity and visual realism. PoseDreamer achieves performance comparable to much larger, manually curated synthetic datasets produced at substantially higher cost, and, when combined, outperforms any other dataset combination. Examples of the generated images and annotations are shown in Figure 2. The contributions of this work are the following: Precise Controllable Human Generation: We develop novel approach for generating photorealistic human images with exact 3D pose control by introducing an enhanced mesh-to-RGB encoding scheme and employing Direct Preference Optimization to align the control model for improved 3D-2D consistency. Curriculum-Based Generation with Model Feedback: We introduce two-stage pipeline incorporating feedback from downstream mesh recovery models to prioritize challenging samples through hard sample mining, ensuring maximum learning value while avoiding redundant easy cases. Large-Scale Synthetic Dataset We introduce synthetic dataset with 500,000 images, each annotated with detailed 3D body mesh. This dataset provides rich and diverse representation for training and evaluating human mesh recovery models. Synthetic Data Analysis: The experiments validate our synthetic datas quality, accuracy, and real-world generalization. We show that the model trained on our synthetic dataset achieves results comparable to or better than those of models trained on datasets obtained from 3D game engines. We also show that combining PoseDreamer with synthetic datasets further improves performance over mixing real-world and synthetic datasets, demonstrating the complementary nature of our dataset. This shows that affordable generation with generative models can replace expensive synthetic humandata acquisition.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: We divide the literature review into related methods on data generation with diffusion models and an overview of datasets for human mesh recovery. Data Generation with Diffusion Models: Diffusion models [8, 24], have transformed data generation methods by enabling the creation of high-quality, diverse synthetic datasets. Recently, [2, 47, 4 L. Prospero et al. Fig. 2: Examples from the PoseDreamer Dataset: High-quality synthetic human samples generated using our pipeline. The examples highlight photorealistic appearance, precise spatial control, and diverse, challenging scenarios that support robust model training. 49, 53, 62] improved the performance of classification models by generating synthetic data with latent diffusion models [46]. However, the methods are limited to image classification. Diffumask [59] and DatasetDM [58] utilize large diffusion models to simultaneously generate synthetic images and annotations for semantic segmentation or depth prediction tasks. Conditioning on text embeddings from large language models [44] provides mechanism to encode information outside of the original data distribution. Still, the methods do require finetuning on every specific dataset. Instance Augmentation [22] generates separate objects in an image, providing framework to augment and anonymize datasets. VGGHeads [21] introduces first large-scale dataset for human-centric tasks generated with diffusion models. Yet, the data generation process simply predicts ground-truth annotations using state-of-the-art methods, thereby limiting performance. In contrast, we condition the generation process on the label, ensuring high consistency between the image and annotation. Similar to our approach, HumanWild [9] constructs diffusion-based synthetic dataset for human pose estimation. However, conditioning on surface normals degrades image quality and necessitates normals for background generation, thereby constraining flexibility. Additionally, Hayakawa et al. [12] propose generated dataset for egocentric 3D human pose estimation. Real-World HMR Datasets: Existing datasets with 3D human annotations face significant limitations despite using sophisticated acquisition devices such as RGBD cameras, LIDAR scanners, or inertial measurement units (IMU). Human3.6M [15] captures 3.6 million poses using motion capture systems but restricts data to 11 actors performing seventeen scenarios in controlled enviPoseDreamer 5 ronments. Similarly, 3DPW [34] provides in-the-wild videos with 3D annotations from video and IMU data, but contains only 60 sequences with limited diversity. MPI-INF-3DHP [36] offers 1.3 million frames from 14 synchronized cameras in laboratory settings. However, these datasets provide only skeletal information without body-shape details, and their controlled acquisition methods severely restrict scenario diversity, clothing variation, and lighting conditions. Several datasets provide full avatar reconstruction of the human body obtained either from complex and expensive capturing systems - CAPE [29] or EHF [42], and have the same pitfalls as the datasets with 3D pose, or use pseudo ground truth data optimized from visual cues, and often suffer from severe inaccuracies. An example of the latter is NeuralAnnot [38,39], which labels avatars using ground-truth annotations, such as 2D and 3D joints, available in other datasets. 4D-DRESS [57] pairs high-quality 4D body scans with garment meshes and pervertex semantic labels to capture clothing dynamics, but its diversity is limited to 64 outfits across 32 subjects. Synthetic HMR Datasets: Advanced graphics pipelines and renders of human 3D models are another promising approach for collecting large 3D annotated datasets. Some available datasets, like AGORA [41], Surreal [55], PeopleSansPeople [7] provide hundreds of thousands of fully annotated images. Ultrapose [60] uses commercial software to generate higher-quality images, capture 1 billion points, and map these images to 3D avatars for DensePose estimation. However, the dataset is not publicly available, which limits its application. BEDLAM [3] is large-scale synthetic dataset produced using physically plausible cloth simulations and artist-generated textures, which requires substantial monetary and computational investment. In contrast, SynBody [61] employs an automated pipeline that combines SMPL-X with procedural garments, hair, accessories, and textures to generate layered human models at scale, thereby reducing both time and economic cost. However, this automation comes at the expense of realism. Concurrently, BEDLAM2 [52] was released, introducing more realistic and diverse camera motion than BEDLAM. Since the data were released only one week before the submission deadline, we cannot include them in our comparisons. In contrast, we introduce method that efficiently scales to an arbitrary number of samples and models wide variety of in-the-wild scenes without manually collecting large library of textures or prompts.

sections.title: 3 Dataset
sections.content: Our objective is to construct large-scale dataset defined as: = {(xi, yi)}m i=1, where each pair consists of an RGB image xi RHW 3 and its corresponding ground truth SMPL-X avatar parameters yi. Following the established paradigm in human mesh estimation [4], most methods employ top-down approach: human detection using off-the-shelf detectors, followed by avatar parameter es6 L. Prospero et al. Fig. 3: Data Generation. Our pipeline begins with SMPL-X [42] parameter and caption generation from multiple sources, followed by curriculum-based hard sample mining to select challenging poses, and concludes with DPO-aligned controllable image generation and comprehensive quality filtering. timation on tightly cropped regions around detected persons. We adopt this framework and focus on single-person mesh recovery. straightforward approach would involve generating images {xi}m i=1 using latent diffusion models [46], then applying state-of-the-art mesh recovery models to predict corresponding labels {yi}m i=1. However, this strategy has fundamental limitation: current methods lack 3D-2D consistency. When projected onto the original images, the predicted 3D meshes are frequently misaligned with the actual human poses and body shapes. This prevents the use of predictions as reliable ground-truth labels for downstream model training. To address this limitation, we propose an inverted generation approach. Instead of generating images first, we begin by sampling diverse SMPL-X [42] avatar parameters {yi}m i=1 and utilize these as conditioning signals for image generation. Combined with control-model alignment and extensive filtering, this method ensures consistency between the generated images and their associated 3D body parameters. Examples of diverse scenarios generated using the same control are shown in Figure 1, while our complete data generation pipeline is illustrated in Figure 3.

sections.title: Label Generation
sections.content: We generate set of 3D body model parameters [42] along with detailed captions that describe the corresponding 3D body poses. SMPL-X models human body, hand, and face geometries using unified parameterization. Specifically, the parameters include pose parameters θ R553 encompassing body, hand, eye, and jaw poses; joint body, hand, and face shape parameters β R10; and facial expression parameters ψ R10. The joint regressor obtains 3D keypoints from parameters via Rθ(J(β)), where Rθ represents the transformation function along the kinematic tree. PoseDreamer 7 simple approach would randomly sample parameters and condition the generation model on these samples. However, for robust performance and strong generalization, the dataset must contain diverse, realistic scenes with varying interactions, occlusions, and backgrounds, and include wide range of plausible human 3D poses. To satisfy both requirements, we sample from two complementary sources: LAION [48]: This large-scale real-world dataset contains over 5B images with diverse scenes and setups, providing sufficient realism and scene diversity but limited pose variety, with predominantly simple standing, sitting, or walking poses. The pipeline begins with YOLO [16] human detection to identify and crop individual persons from the images. We then employ the Gemma3 vision-language model [51] to generate detailed captions that describe the visual content, including clothing, environment, and overall scene context. For 3D parameter extraction, we apply combination of SMPLer-X [4] and Token-HMR [6] models to predict SMPL-X parameters from the cropped human regions. We deliberately combine predictions from both models to mitigate potential bias that could arise from relying on single downstream model for 3D mesh parameter prediction. This approach helps ensure more robust and reliable ground truth. AMASS [32]: This extensive database contains over 40 hours of diverse human motion-capture data, providing the challenging, dynamic pose diversity that LAION inherently lacks. The dataset includes complex activities such as dancing, martial arts, sports movements, and everyday interactions that exhibit significant joint articulation and body deformation. For each motion sequence, we sample representative frames and use Gemma [51] to generate detailed captions that include specific body-pose descriptions and comprehensive scene descriptions that incorporate diverse environmental elements, lighting conditions, clothing styles, and background settings. The VLM [51] is specifically tasked with describing human pose and generating realistic, detailed scene context, ensuring that the resulting images exhibit wide visual variety while preserving the complex pose structure of the original motion capture data.

sections.title: Image Generation
sections.content: To make ground truth sample parameters compatible with state-of-the-art control models [64] and to enforce robust spatial control, we render 3D meshes as RGB images. Common approaches for this conversion include DensePose [10], which maps body surface coordinates to 2D texture space, and Continuous Surface Embeddings [40], which encode surface correspondences through learned embeddings that maintain consistency across different poses and body shapes. However, we found that simple mapping of vertex IDs to RGB colors yields insufficient visual variation across poses, particularly for distinguishing head orientations and body configurations. This limited color variation prevents generative models from incorporating detailed 3D spatial information during conditioning. Following PNCC notation [65] to address this limitation, we propose an improved color coding scheme normalizing each spatial axis (X, Y, Z) independently and mapping the normalized coordinates directly to RGB channels. 8 L. Prospero et al. Compared to naive mapping, it provides richer visual cues that better encode spatial relationships and pose variations. Our experimental evaluation demonstrates that models trained with this enhanced mesh-to-RGB mapping achieve significantly more precise 3D pose generation than baseline color-coding approaches, thereby validating the effectiveness of our spatial encoding strategy. For the control training, we collect dataset of 130,000 rendered 3D meshes with their corresponding ground-truth images. To ensure accurate alignment between the rendered meshes and images, we leverage annotations from two existing datasets: DensePoseCOCO [10] and AGORA [41]. Using this dataset, we train spatial control LoRA [13] following the training procedure of EasyControl [64]. This approach enables the training of well-aligned control mechanism for image generation. It also provides the flexibility to combine multiple independently trained LoRAs, allowing for more versatile and compositional control. Leveraging the multi-conditioning capability of the EasyControl inference pipeline, we incorporate the 2D pose skeleton as an additional conditioning input, thereby improving consistency between generated images and their corresponding 3D pose annotations.

sections.title: 3.2 Control Model Alignment
sections.content: While EasyControl [64] provides mechanism for conditioning image generation on 3D mesh inputs, it represents weak conditioning method that operates through cross-attention mechanisms without explicit spatial constraints. The model learns to associate control signals with visual patterns during training, but lacks architectural guarantees to enforce precise adherence to the provided 3D pose information, often resulting in generated images that deviate from the intended body configurations. However, we can effectively detect such misalignments using robust 2D proxy metrics. State-of-the-art 2D joint regressors [33] are significantly more robust than their 3D counterparts, as they are trained on larger datasets and tackle fundamentally easier task of 2D keypoint localization. Our evaluation setup proceeds as follows: given generated image and the corresponding groundtruth SMPL-X parameters used for conditioning, we first reproject the SMPL-X models 3D joints to obtain the ground-truth 2D keypoints. We then apply 2D joint regressor to the generated image to predict 2D keypoints, and compute the Object Keypoint Similarity (OKS) score between the predicted and reprojected keypoints: OKS = (cid:80) exp (cid:17) (cid:16) d2 2s2κ2 δ(vi > 0) (cid:80) δ(vi > 0) , (1) where di is the Euclidean distance between predicted and ground truth keypoints, is the object scale, κi are per-keypoint constants, and vi indicates keypoint visibility. This 2D evaluation approach reliably measures pose correspondence while being significantly more stable than direct 3D mesh regression methods, making it ideal for assessing control fidelity. This capability to reliably PoseDreamer 9 Fig. 4: DPO Alignment Effectiveness: Comparison between baseline control model (top row) and DPO-aligned model (bottom row) using identical SMPL-X parameters and captions. The aligned model generates more consistent poses with greater adherence to the ground truth 3D parameters, demonstrating improved control precision and reduced pose deviations. measure control alignment enables us to employ Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) [45, 56] to systematically improve the conditioning mechanism. We can enhance control precision and overall generation quality by optimizing the control model to favor generations that exhibit higher pose alignment scores. We construct preference pairs by ranking generated images according to their OKS scores relative to the ground truth SMPL-X parameters. Images with higher alignment scores are treated as preferred samples (xw 0 ), while those with lower scores become less preferred samples (xl 0), where xw 0 xl 0. Following the Flow-DPO framework [28], we optimize control model with: (cid:20) (cid:18) log sigmoid (cid:19)(cid:21) (Lw Ll) βt 2 (2) with Lw = vw vw θ 2 vw vw ref2, Ll = vl vl θ2 vl vl ref2 , where represents the 3D mesh control condition, βt = β(1 t2), and denotes the velocity field for rectified flow models. This objective encourages the control model to generate images that more accurately reflect the input 3D pose constraints, thereby improving control precision and overall generation quality. We use Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) [13] with rank = 128 and α = 128. This strategy provides the additional advantage of memory efficiency by eliminating the need to store the reference model separately, as the base model with the adapter disabled naturally serves as pref during training. Training is conducted with batch size of 32 for 6,400 iterations using the Adam optimizer with learning rate of 5 106. The complete fine-tuning process requires 2 days on 4 A6000 GPUs. We validate the effectiveness of our DPO alignment on held-out test set by measuring control precision using OKS scores between generated images and ground truth 2D keypoints. The baseline control model achieves an OKS of 0.874 0.103, while our DPO-aligned model reaches 0.927 0.052. This repre10 L. Prospero et al. sents substantial 42.1% reduction in the error rate, demonstrating significant improvements in pose-alignment consistency and reduced variance in generation quality. We show examples of the improved OKS alignment in Fig. 4.

sections.title: 3.3 Hard Sample Mining
sections.content: Given an aligned control model and an extensive library of 3D body parameters with corresponding captions, we can generate datasets of arbitrary size. However, naive random sampling often yields redundant samples with similar poses and scene configurations, which fail to provide informative training signals. We propose curriculum-based sample selection strategy that prioritizes challenging examples through two-stage generation pipeline to address this limitation and maximize data efficiency. Stage 1: Baseline Generation. We randomly sample parameter vectors from our data sources to generate an initial dataset using the aligned control model. We then train an SMPL-X regressor [4] on this initial dataset and evaluate its performance on held-out test set. Performance is measured using OKS scores computed between the regressors predictions and the ground truth 2D keypoints obtained from robust 2D joint detectors. Lower OKS scores indicate more challenging samples, where the current mesh recovery model performs poorly. Stage 2: Difficulty-Aware Generation. We leverage the observed relationship between SMPL-X parameters and task difficulty by training gradient boosting decision tree regressor [19] to predict OKS scores directly from parameter vectors. This regressor enables us to rank candidate samples by their expected complexity level without requiring full image generation and evaluation. We then select the top samples with the lowest predicted OKS scores, corresponding to the most challenging configurations, and use our aligned control model to generate the final dataset. This curriculum-based approach ensures that computational resources are allocated to generating samples that provide maximum learning value, focusing on challenging scenarios that improve model robustness while eliminating redundant, easy cases that contribute minimal training signal.

sections.title: 3.4 Dataset Filtering
sections.content: Despite generating high-quality, aligned control models, failure cases inevitably arise, yielding imperfect samples that can degrade training quality. To ensure robust data quality, we implement comprehensive three-stage filtering pipeline that addresses the primary failure modes we observe in generated data: crowded scenes and severe occlusions, general pose misalignment, and 3D head pose inconsistencies. Crowded Scenes: We run YOLO [16] on generated images and filter out samples containing more than five detected persons. This threshold removes only PoseDreamer 11 Fig. 5: OKS scores: Comparison between images with low and high OKS values. Only images with an OKS score greater than 0.8 pass the filtering stage. highly crowded scenes in which pose estimation quality cannot be reliably ensured, thereby allowing the model to learn partial occlusions and human-tohuman interactions. General Misalignment: We employ the same OKS-based evaluation framework used in control model alignment to filter out samples with poor pose correspondence. Images with OKS scores below predefined threshold are discarded, some examples are shown in Fig. 5. The combination of alignment and filtering is necessary because control model alignment provides key advantages: (a) computational efficiency - aligned models have significantly higher filtering pass rate, reducing wasted computation on poor samples; (b) quality improvement - samples that pass the filtering threshold exhibit better overall alignment when generated from aligned models; and (c) bias mitigation - unaligned models tend to bias toward simple poses that achieve consistency, potentially excluding challenging poses from the final filtered dataset that are essential for robust training. 3D Head Pose Inconsistency: We extract 3D head pose parameters (roll, pitch, yaw) from the ground truth SMPL-X parameters and use VGGHeads [21] to predict head pose from generated images. Samples with mean absolute error (MAE) above threshold in any rotation axis are filtered out, ensuring head pose consistency in challenging out-of-distribution poses where general body alignment metrics may be insufficient. This multi-stage filtering approach ensures that only high-quality, consistent samples contribute to the final training dataset while maintaining diversity in challenging scenarios. The final dataset consists of 500,000 samples, filtered from 800,000 initially generated images, with 500,000 generated from LAION [48] poses and 300,000 from AMASS [32] poses. The full generation process required 6,700 GPU hours.

sections.content: We evaluate our approach along three dimensions: downstream performance on standard human mesh recovery benchmarks, image-quality comparison with existing synthetic datasets, and ablation analysis of our pipeline components. 12 L. sections.title: 4.1 Single Human Mesh Recovery Benchmarking
sections.content: We extensively evaluate our dataset using the benchmarking protocol introduced by SMPLer-X [4]. Following their comprehensive evaluation framework, we train the SMPLer-X-S model on our synthetic data and assess its generalization across multiple real-world benchmarks with ground-truth SMPL-X annotations. Within this evaluation framework, we compare the performance of models trained on single datasets and mixture of datasets. We evaluate on five academic datasets for human mesh recovery evaluation: AGORA [41], synthetic benchmark for rendering engine evaluation; UBody [26], covering 15 real-world scenarios with diverse in-the-wild captures; EgoBody [63], large-scale first-person view dataset; 3DPW [34], providing challenging outdoor sequences; and EHF [42], laboratory dataset with 100 diverse poses. These benchmarks provide comprehensive evaluation across synthetic and real-world scenarios, including controlled and uncontrolled environments, and diverse viewpoints, enabling thorough assessment of the effectiveness of our synthetic data for real-world generalization. Experimental Results: Table 1 presents comprehensive evaluation results across five diverse benchmarks spanning real-world and synthetic scenarios. Our diffusion-generated dataset achieves strong performance across all evaluations and yields results comparable to BEDLAM [3], which is 2 larger and more expensive to design. Notably, our dataset outperforms all real-world datasets, showPoseDreamer 13 Table 2: Image Quality: Diffusiongenerated data achieves superior realism than rendering-based synthetic datasets. Dataset Inception Score FID PoseDreamer BEDLAM [3] SynBody [61] AGORA [41] 9.78 4.35 5.39 5.55 1.72 4.55 5.19 8.14 Table 3: PVE () results for models trained on PoseDreamer and BEDLAM on in-the-wild benchmarks. Dataset UBody [26] MPII [1] MSCOCO [27] PoseDreamer BEDLAM [3] 97.6 146.3 122.3 141.1 129.4 163. ing that their pseudo-ground-truth annotations are noisier than those of PoseDreamer. When combined with BEDLAM, our dataset outperforms all multidataset mixtures, including BEDLAM+COCO and the five-dataset ensemble. We attribute this synergy to complementary strengths: BEDLAM contributes precise 3D supervision via rendering, while our diffusion-generated images contribute photorealistic diversity that rendered pipelines cannot easily replicate. These results position diffusion-based generation as scalable and effective alternative to both real and rendered data sources. 4.

sections.title: Image Quality Evaluation
sections.content: A critical advantage of our approach is the substantial improvement in visual realism compared to traditional rendering engine datasets. As shown in Tab. 2, PoseDreamer achieves an Inception Score of 9.78 and FID of 1.72, significantly outperforming established synthetic datasets including BEDLAM [3] (IS: 4.35, FID: 4.55), SynBody [61] (IS: 5.39, FID: 5.19), and AGORA [41] (IS: 5.55, FID: 8.14). This represents 76% improvement in Inception Score and 69% reduction in FID compared to the best-performing baseline (AGORA). The superior metrics reflect the photorealism capabilities of state-of-the-art diffusion models such as FLUX [24], which have been trained on vast collections of real-world imagery and can generate images that are nearly indistinguishable from real photos. This enhanced realism directly reduces the domain gap when deploying models trained on synthetic data to real-world scenarios, addressing fundamental limitation of rendering-engine approaches, which often produce images with distinctive synthetic appearance. To further validate this, we compare the model trained on our dataset with the model trained on BEDLAM  (Table 1)  on two additional in-the-wild benchmarks: MPII [1] and MSCOCO [27]. The model trained on PoseDreamer significantly outperforms the model trained on BEDLAM. The results are reported in Table 3.

sections.title: 4.3 Ablation Study
sections.content: To evaluate the impact of our pipeline design choices, we compare the downstream model performance on the test set of UBody [26], EgoBody [63], and 14 L. Prospero et al. Table 4: Ablations: We evaluate the impact of our pipeline components based on their effect on downstream model performance. Dataset PoseDreamer w/o Filtering w/o AMASS [32] data w/o Hard Sample Mining UBody PVE 104.43 111.07 105.26 107.93 Egobody PVE 128.02 130.66 132.96 131.20 EHF PVE 95.08 95.36 103.54 95. EHF [42]. We compare the performance of SMPLer-X [4], trained for 6 epochs on datasets generated with different versions of our pipeline, and report the average PVE metric; the results are shown in Tab. 4. Removing any component of our pipeline degrades downstream mesh recovery performance, validating the necessity of each stage. The quality filtering step prevents training on misaligned samples, AMASS [32] integration adds essential pose complexity, and hard sample mining ensures the model learns from the most challenging configurations.

sections.title: 5 Limitations and Broader Impact
sections.content: PoseDreamer offers complementary alternative to rendering engine datasets with distinct trade-offs. While rendering engines provide high-quality labels, our diffusion-based approach offers greater flexibility, scalability, and photorealism, albeit at the cost of some label precision. Despite extensive alignment and filtering, generated samples may contain subtle inconsistencies that rendering engines avoid. The fundamental trade-off between perfect synthetic labels and realistic visual diversity is key choice in dataset construction, and we demonstrate that the benefits of enhanced realism and scalability often outweigh the costs of reduced label precision in many applications. Our reliance on existing foundation models may also introduce biases from their training data, which could limit pose diversity for underrepresented demographic groups or activities. Generating realistic human imagery raises ethical concerns about potential misuse for misleading content or unauthorized representations. However, our focus on 3D mesh parameters rather than identity-specific features helps preserve privacy by preventing the identifiable replication of individuals. Our scalable approach democratizes access to high-quality human pose datasets, advancing research in motion analysis, healthcare, and assistive technologies while benefiting researchers who lack access to expensive motion capture systems.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: PoseDreamer demonstrates that diffusion-based synthetic data generation can be viable and scalable alternative to traditional rendering pipelines for human PoseDreamer 15 mesh recovery tasks. Combining the photorealism and scene diversity of generative models with robust alignment and filtering mechanisms, we bridge the gap between synthetic data quality and real-world applicability. This work opens new avenues for democratizing access to high-quality 3D human datasets, enabling researchers to advance human recognition and reconstruction methods without the substantial computational and financial costs of traditional synthetic data generation.

sections.title: 7 Acknowledgments
sections.content: This work was funded by The Podium Institute for Sports Medicine and Technology, at the University of Oxford. O.K. is supported by Google unrestricted gift. J.H. acknowledges the generous support of Toyota Motor Europe (TME) and EPSRC (VisualAI, EP/T028572/1). C.R. is supported by ERC StG 101222037Volute.).

sections.title: A Additional Analisys
sections.content: A.1 Scaling We evaluate PoseDreamers performance on larger models by training several versions of SMPLer-X-L [4], ViT-L-based model. The results are shown in Table 5. The model trained on 0.75M samples from two datasets (PoseDreamer and BEDLAM) outperforms the SMPLer-X-L model trained on mixture of five datasets across all benchmarks, except EgoBody and AGORA (the latter included in the five-dataset train set). Table 5: Scaling. We train multiple versions of the SMPLer-X-L and SMPLer-X-S models to demonstrate that PoseDreamer contributes to performance improvements when scaling both data and model size. #Datasets #Inst. Datasets Backbone AGORA EgoBody UBody 3DPW EHF PVE PVE PVE MPJPE PVE 2 5 2 2 5 2 10 0.75M Ours + BEDLAM (50-50 split) ViT-S ViT-S SMPLer-X-S 5 datasets 0.75M 0.75M Ours + BEDLAM (500K-250K) ViT-L 0.75M Ours + BEDLAM (50-50 split) ViT-L ViT-L SMPLer-X-L 5 datasets 0.75M 1.5M Ours + BEDLAM (50-50 split) ViT-L ViT-L 1.5M SMPLer-X-L 10 datasets 156.8 119.0 138.7 138.1 88.3 131.3 82.6 106.1 114.2 101.1 114.2 98. 99.6 69.7 97.6 110.1 93.8 107.4 110.8 94.6 104.0 96.8 110.2 88.5 100.1 97. 85.6 82.5 84.3 100.5 73.3 93.9 89.5 67.5 64.0 Notably, the model trained on an equal split of PoseDreamer and BEDLAM underperforms compared to the model trained on 250K samples from BEDLAM and 500K samples from PoseDreamer. This indicates that the diversity and photorealism of PoseDreamer improve performance as data size increases. We also compare the SMPLer-X-L model trained on 1.5M samples from two datasets (PoseDreamer and BEDLAM) with version trained on mixture of ten datasets. The results are comparable across all benchmarks that are not part of the training set (AGORA and EgoBody). Moreover, the model trained on 2 datasets outperforms the one trained on 10 datasets on the in-the-wild UBody benchmark. These results demonstrate that our dataset provides substantial diversity in poses and environments, leading to improved model performance when scaling both model size and the number of training samples. A.2 Domain-specific Generation Compared to traditional datasets, PoseDreamer offers the flexibility to generate images tailored to specific domain. To evaluate this capability, we generate 30K images using poses from the MOYO dataset [54]. Examples of the generated images are shown in Figure 6. Table 6: Domain-Specific Generation. We train two versions of the SMPLer-X model: one using 300K images sampled from random poses, and another in which 30K images are replaced with domain-specific yoga poses. The model trained with yoga poses decisively outperforms the baseline on the MPII Yoga subset used for testing. PoseDreamer Dataset UBody 3DPW EHF MPII (YOGA subset) PVE PVE PVE PVE 270K random + 30K yoga poses 97.0 112.7 105.3 113.3 104.8 300K random 97.4 171.1 199. Fig. 6: Yoga Poses Generated. Example of images generated using yoga poses from the MOYO [54] dataset. Fig. 7: Trained Models Comparison. Visual comparison of predictions from models trained with and without yoga poses. From left to right: original image, prediction of the model trained without yoga poses, and prediction of the model trained with yoga poses. The model trained with yoga poses produces predictions that are visually more consistent with the ground-truth pose. We then train two versions of the SMPLer-X-S model: one using 300K randomly sampled images from AMASS and LAION poses, and another in which 30K of these images are replaced with generated yoga-pose images. We evaluate both models on UBody, 3DPW, EHF, and on the 487 instances of MPII whose activity description contains the word yoga". The results are reported in Table 6. The model trained with 10% yoga poses achieves performance comparable to the baseline on all benchmarks, except for the MPII Yoga subset, where it decisively outperforms the baseline. This improvement is further supported by visual comparison of the predictions from the two models on the MPII subset, as shown in Figure 7. 22 L. Prospero et al. These results demonstrate that our pipeline is flexible and can generate data for specific domains of interest, thereby improving downstream performance in those domains. A.3 Spatial Control Ablations To train reliable control mechanism for dataset sample generation, we experimented with different representations of the control image. Our initial attempt involved generating imagelabel pairs by running pretrained DensePose [10] model on subset of LAION [48] images. However, these predictions were often inaccurate or incomplete, or had low-quality boundaries, resulting in poor pixel alignment for our control mechanism. We next considered rendering DensePoseCTable 7: Control Representations. We compare the effect of different representation choices for the control model training. The comparison is based on downstream model performance. Control 2D Representation UBody Egobody EHF PVE PVE PVE PoseDreamer (PNCC) CSE [40] colormap Densepose predictions 104.43 128.02 95.08 130.66 130.71 129.83 153.10 151.40 119.95 OCO [10] and AGORA [41] ground truth annotations to ensure high-quality image pairs. Initially, we rendered SMPL-X parameters using the colormap from Continuous Surface Embeddings [40]. This colormap, however, maps each vertex to value between 0 and 1, which provides insufficient visual variation across poses, particularly for distinguishing head orientations or body configurations. Moreover, this colormap is only defined for SMPL parameters, whereas our goal is to model the full body pose using the SMPL-X model [42]. We were able to obtain stable and reliable control using the PNCC colormap [65], and mapping each X,Y,Z normalized coordinate to R,G,B values. Following the ablations from the main paper, we compare different representation choices for the conditioning image and report their impact on downstream model performance in Table 7. Using high-quality image pairs and more detailed colormap significantly improves downstream model performance. A.4 Filtering Statistics To ensure the quality of the generated dataset, we introduce filtering pipeline. We provide additional statistics on the scores of the generated images. Figure 8 shows the histogram of the OKS score distribution for pose predictions on 60,000 generated images, along with examples of lowand high-scoring images. In addition, we present the histogram of the distribution of mean absolute errors PoseDreamer (MAE) for roll, pitch, and yaw in the 3D head pose parameter predictions. The thresholds used for the final dataset selection are 0.8 for the OKS score and 25 for the head pose error. A.

sections.title: Image Quality
sections.content: To quantitatively evaluate the synthetic-to-real domain gap, we compute the quality and coverage of samples produced by generative model following [23], and compare them to the faces subset of the LAION dataset [48]. The results are shown in Figure 9. We observe that the generated images closely match the real distribution, whereas rendered datasets achieve much lower F-score. Furthermore, UMAP [35] and t-SNE [31] projections of DINO-v3 [50] and CLIP [44] image features show that PoseDreamer samples occupy the same region of the real data manifold. In comparison, BEDLAM [3], AGORA [41], and SynBody [61] form isolated clusters. A.6 2D Reprojection Our synthetic datasets precise ground truth annotations enable the incorporation of additional explicit supervision signals within the SMPL-X topology. We explore how rich synthetic data can improve mesh recovery through enhanced architectural supervision. We introduce minor modification to the SMPLer-X architecture by adding intermediate visual marker predictions. While SMPLerX originally predicts 3D joint locations as input to pose parameter heads, we extend this concept by incorporating visual markers as sparse set of 64 fixed vertices on the mesh surface, following VirtualMarker [30]. Unlike joints, which primarily capture pose information, visual markers are more sensitive to shape variations, providing valuable signals for predicting shape parameters. This apTable 8: 2D Reprojection Error Analysis. PoseDreamer provides rich supervision via visual markers, achieving 2D reprojection accuracy approaching that of models trained on 32 datasets while using only our single synthetic source. Training Data EHF EgoBody 2D Reproj 2D Reproj 2D Reproj UBody 32 Datasets PoseDreamer PoseDreamer + Markers 0.013 0.017 0.015 0.026 0.027 0. 0.024 0.030 0.025 proach provides shape-sensitive supervision while maintaining efficiency through sparse representation. Our modified architecture predicts both joints and visual markers simultaneously, feeding marker predictions to the shape parameter head as additional input. We apply supervision losses to markers in both 3D and 2D spaces, directly on predicted markers and after reconstructing them from predicted avatars, analogous to joint supervision in the original architecture. Table 8 24 L. Prospero et al. Fig. 8: Filtering statistics. Distribution of the pose OKS errors, and head pose MAE for roll pitch yaw, together with examples of lowand high-scoring images. PoseDreamer 25 Fig. 9: Image Quality Analysis. Results for quality and coverage of the samples following [23]. UMAP [35] and TSNE [31] projections of DINO [50] and CLIP [44] image features for PoseDreamer compared to real images from the LAION faces dataset and other synthetic datasets. Samples from our generated dataset more closely follow the distribution of the real dataset. 26 L. Prospero et al. demonstrates the effectiveness of this enhanced supervision. Our model with visual marker supervision achieves 2D reprojection errors approaching SMPLer-X trained on 32 combined real and synthetic datasets, demonstrating that highquality synthetic supervision can achieve competitive performance with significantly fewer data sources. Fig. 10: PoseDreamer Failure cases. There are some failure cases for our method. These include unrealistic body shapes for complex input poses or input descriptions that involve complex interactions with other people. Additionally, it can happen that the model fails to understand the 3D pose perspective of the input, or that it places the person in an unrealistic scenario (for example, floating with no support).

sections.title: B Failure Cases
sections.content: While our method is effective, it has limitations: the generated images are not always fully photorealistic or perfectly aligned with the control input. Most mistakes are filtered out during the filtering stage, but minor ones occasionally slip through. Common failure cases include complex body poses, such as yoga positions or intricate interactions between multiple people described in the text. In these cases, the generated images occasionally include deformed body parts or additional limbs. Other frequent issues arise from physically implausible scenarios, such as people appearing to float. In some cases, the model also struggles with the 3D-to-2D mapping of limb joints, leading to artifacts such as legs appearing bent in the generated image even though they are straight in the control input, as the model incorrectly adjusts them for image perspective. We show some examples of failure cases in Figure 10. Mitigating Distribution Gap in Diffusion-Generated

sections.title: Data
sections.content: Diffusion models trained on large-scale web datasets like LAION often exhibit aesthetic biases, resulting in oversaturated, heavily filtered imagery with an artificial "AI look." This happens because these models learn to copy the visual FLUX.1-Dev +Realism LoRA FLUX.1-Dev +Realism LoRA PoseDreamer Fig. 11: Effectiveness of Realism Adapter. We show comparisons of the same image generated without (left) and with the realism LoRA adapter. Images generated without it tend to be more oversaturated, giving an "AI look". styles common in retouched internet images, which are quite different from natural image distributions, leading the generated synthetic data to look unrealistic. To address this distribution gap, we use specialized LoRA adapter [13] in our pipeline, trained on real-world images to reduce aesthetic artifacts from foundation models. This adapter learns to transform images from the biased aesthetic style of web-trained diffusion models toward more natural image appearance. The modular design of EasyControl [64] allows to combine the aesthetic/style adapter with our spatial control LoRA at inference time, producing images that maintain both precise spatial consistency with control inputs and improved realistic appearance. Figure 11 demonstrates the effectiveness of this approach, showing substantial reduction in oversaturation and artificial enhancement artifacts. This aesthetic correction represents crucial step in bridging the domain gap between synthetic training data and real-world deployment scenarios. 28 L. Prospero et al. Control Model Alignment: Implementation Details For DPO training, we generate 40,000 preference pairs by creating 4 image variants for each caption and 3D mesh combination, then ranking them based on OKS scores to establish preferred and less preferred samples. We use lower β value of 250 compared to the original Flow-DPO [28] value of 500, as we find that control models require less regularization due to their more constrained generation space. Additionally, traditional ControlNet approaches may face challenges with RL-based alignment, as both the control mechanism and alignment procedures operate on diffusion model activations, potentially leading to optimization conflicts that can compromise training stability. In contrast, EasyControl [64] modular adapter architecture we use provides cleaner separation between control and alignment components, facilitating more stable joint optimization.

sections.title: E Additional Visualizations
sections.content: We provide some additional examples of dataset samples in Figure 12. Fig. 12: PoseDreamer Dataset Samples. Examples from PoseDreamer dataset showing diverse poses, scenes, and high-quality image generation with precise 3D mesh correspondence.
